front: Which two primary String class methods are used to change the case of characters in Java?
Do the toUpperCase() and toLowerCase() methods modify the original String object?
back: No. Strings are immutable in Java. These methods return a new String object with the changed case, leaving the original string unchanged.

front: How do the toUpperCase() and toLowerCase() methods handle locale-specific case conversions?
back: They have overloaded versions that take a Locale object (e.g., toUpperCase(Locale locale)) to ensure correct case mapping for language-specific rules, which is crucial for internationalization.

front: What is the purpose of the Character class methods toUpperCase(char ch) and toLowerCase(char ch) in this context?
back: They are used to change the case of individual primitive char values, which can be useful for processing characters one at a time within a string or building a new string character-by-character.

front: Why is it important to specify a Locale when using toUpperCase() or toLowerCase() for user-facing text?
back: Using the default locale can lead to unexpected results. For example, the Turkish locale ('tr') has different case mapping rules for 'i' and 'I'. Specifying a locale ensures consistent and correct behavior across all environments.
